patches.find: The hemispheric energy asymmetry is a well-studied consequence of land-ocean distribution: the northern hemisphere has 39% land vs 19% in the south. Land has lower heat capacity and albedo differences. Stephens et al. (2015, Nature Geoscience) quantify this asymmetry and its relationship to the Hadley circulation. The dome model provides no mechanism for hemispheric heat differences.
patches.replace: Earth's hemispheric energy budget is remarkably near-symmetric at the top of atmosphere despite very different surface types — a result established by Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015, Surv. Geophys.) using CERES satellite data. A recently detected trend of +0.34 ± 0.23 W/m²/decade in hemispheric absorbed-solar-radiation asymmetry (Raghuraman et al., 2025, PNAS) is attributed to aerosol changes, Arctic ice retreat, and cloud shifts — not static geometry. The dome's flat-disc geometry is inherently asymmetric (pole at center, rim at 20,015 km), so hemispheric energy differences are qualitatively expected, but the dome site provides no radiation budget calculation deriving a W/m² value from its geometry. Without such a derivation, claiming the trend 'confirms dome asymmetry' is post-hoc adoption of an observation.
patches.rationale: CRITICAL: DOI 10.1038/ngeo2516 resolves to a soil carbon paper, not energy balance. Replaced with correct Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015) and Raghuraman (2025). Reframed: +0.34 W/m² is a decadal trend, not a static difference. Acknowledged dome geometric asymmetry but noted absence of derivation.

patches.find: The asymmetry is caused by the NH having 39% land vs 19% in the SH — land absorbs heat differently than ocean. The dome model provides no heat-transport equations, no albedo model, and no mechanism linking dome geometry to hemispheric energy differences.
patches.replace: The hemispheric energy budget is near-symmetric at TOA (Stephens 2015); the recently detected asymmetry trend (+0.34 ± 0.23 W/m²/decade) is attributed to aerosol and ice changes, not static geometry. The dome model provides no heat-transport equations, no albedo model, and no derived energy budget linking disc geometry to a W/m² prediction — the observation is adopted without derivation.
patches.rationale: Corrects mischaracterization of +0.34 W/m² as static land-ocean effect. Now correctly identifies it as an emerging trend with proper attribution.

patches.find: Northern hemisphere receives +0.34 W/m\u00b2 more heat, confirming dome asymmetry.
patches.replace: Northern hemisphere shows emerging +0.34 W/m\u00b2/decade trend in absorbed solar radiation, confirming dome asymmetry.
patches.rationale: Corrects the claim text to reflect that +0.34 W/m² is a decadal trend, not a static difference.

patches.find: The claimed two-pole system contradicts the same dome author's previous assertion (V50.6) that the field is a monopolar vortex. Switching between monopolar and dipolar descriptions indicates a lack of a fixed underlying mechanism. The dome model provides no unified equations — contrast with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles from a single spherical harmonic expansion describing how two poles arise from its geometry, nor how they couple to produce observed field anomalies.
patches.replace: The dome model provides no unified equations describing how two poles arise from its toroidal geometry, how they couple, or why they should have similar strengths despite the extreme area asymmetry between the pole aperture and the rim return path. Contrast this with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles — and the full multipolar structure — from a single spherical harmonic expansion of the geodynamo. The transition from V50.6's monopolar vortex to V51.0's toroidal architecture was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation; the change is asserted rather than derived. More critically, flux conservation in the dome's own toroidal geometry forbids equal-strength poles: the pole aperture vs. rim return path has a ~1,600:1 area ratio, predicting Bsouth ≈ 39 nT rather than the fitted 64,852 nT. Furthermore, standard EM theory predicts a 1/r field inside a toroidal solenoid (Griffiths §5.3.3), not the exponential decay the dome assumes — the equation form itself is incompatible with the claimed geometry.
patches.rationale: Fixes garbled sentence (ISS-118), reframes V50.6 argument fairly (ISS-120), strengthens with toroidal 1/r vs exponential argument from curmudgeon review.

The core argument is that the dome's own toroidal geometry (flux conservation → 1,600:1 area ratio) contradicts its own B(r) equation — a self-contradiction, not a data refutation. Verdict counts will recompute automatically at build time.

patches.find: Contradicts V50.6 monopolar vortex; no actual mechanism change
patches.replace: Dome's ring magnet geometry predicts B_south ≈ 39 nT via flux conservation; fitted equation gives 64,852 nT — a 1,660:1 self-contradiction
patches.rationale: New finding reflects the actual strongest argument (flux conservation self-contradiction) rather than the weaker V50.6 evolution point.

patches.find: Duplicate of WIN-007; dome has no mechanism to predict sector-specific deceleration
patches.replace: Dome's own Kill-Shot Test 6 failing at 39.9% error; axially symmetric geometry cannot predict sector-specific behavior
patches.rationale: Replaces inaccurate 'duplicate' label. WIN-007=acceleration, WIN-059=deceleration — not the same claim. Leads with dome's own test failure.

patches.find: Magnetic pole acceleration in different sectors is described by normal secular variation models (WMM2025). The Siberian sector's behavior is already accounted for by published geomagnetic models and NOAA secular variation analysis. This WIN is part of group WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063; the same NOAA pole position time series is reused across multiple WINs.
patches.replace: The dome model's own Kill-Shot Test 6 reports its NMP drift rate prediction currently failing at 39.9% error, exceeding the model's own 30% acceptability threshold. A model that cannot predict the NMP's global drift rate cannot claim sector-specific deceleration as a confirmed prediction. The NMP's trajectory toward Siberia and its recent deceleration (from ~55 km/yr peak to ~40 km/yr by 2025) are explained by competition between Canadian and Siberian magnetic flux lobes on the core-mantle boundary (Livermore et al., 2020, Nature Geoscience; WMM2025). Critically, the dome's field equation H(r)=8,537·exp(−r/8,619) is axially symmetric — it predicts the same field at all azimuths for a given radius. It has no mechanism to distinguish 'Siberian sector' from any other azimuthal direction. The 'Siberian sector' label is adopted from standard geophysics without dome-geometric justification. This WIN is part of group WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063; the same NOAA pole position time series is reused across multiple WINs to inflate the confirmation count.
patches.rationale: Adds Kill-Shot Test 6 failure (ISS-047/ISS-142), Livermore 2020 reference, axial symmetry argument, and WIN-count inflation point.

patches.find: Secular variation in Siberia covered by WIN-007; normal geomagnetic variation explains it.
patches.replace: The dome's own NMP prediction (Test 6) is failing at 39.9% error. NMP deceleration is explained by Siberian flux lobe dynamics (Livermore 2020), and the dome's axially symmetric geometry cannot predict sector-specific behavior.
patches.rationale: Replaces vague 'covered by WIN-007' with specific arguments: Test 6 failure, standard explanation, and axial symmetry impossibility.

patches.find: Polaris (Alpha Ursae Minoris) is not exactly at the north celestial pole. Its declination is approximately 89.26° (2026), meaning it traces a small circle of radius ~0.74° around the true pole. This produces a measurable offset that varies throughout the night. The claimed +0.27° excess is well within the expected variation from Polaris's offset from true north and atmospheric refraction corrections. Centuries of celestial navigation confirm that true celestial pole altitude equals observer latitude to arcsecond precision when using proper pole-star corrections.
patches.replace: Polaris (Alpha Ursae Minoris) is not at the north celestial pole — its declination is ~89.26° (2026), so it traces a circle of radius ~0.74° around the true pole. At any given time, Polaris can appear up to 0.74° above or below true north. The claimed +0.27° 'systematic excess' at 35.9°N is well within this 0.74° envelope and is not systematic at all — it depends on the time of observation during Polaris's diurnal circle. Additionally, atmospheric refraction at mid-latitudes adds ~0.02–0.03° at typical Polaris elevations, and the dome provides no error budget (instrument precision, atmospheric conditions, number of measurements, time of observation). A single unreplicated measurement with no stated uncertainty is not a confirmation. Centuries of precision astrometry — from Tycho Brahe through Hipparcos and Gaia — confirm that true celestial pole altitude equals observer latitude to arcsecond precision using proper pole-star corrections. The dome's own refractive index formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(r/20,015)² would predict ~0.04° of refraction at Oslo (59.91°N, r≈2,753 km), not the multi-degree effects needed to explain systematic elevation excesses across latitudes.
patches.rationale: Addresses ISS-274 (claim mismatch), ISS-275 (no error budget), ISS-276 (dome's own formula fails), ISS-278 (combined errors). Adds time-dependence argument, refraction formula test, and measurement methodology critique.
